pattern: Because a busy server's next free time only ever moves forward, keeping two ordered sets (servers currently free, servers currently busy ordered by when they free up) turns the round-robin-with-wraparound assignment rule into two searches for the nearest available slot.
hints: At any moment, a server is either free or busy until some known future time; you never need to simulate time minute by minute, only jump to the events (arrivals and completions) that matter.
When a request arrives, its preferred server is (i mod k); if that one is busy, the rule says to search forward, wrapping around, for the next free server, not simply the least-recently-used one.

Keep free server ids in a sorted structure so you can quickly find the smallest free id that is >= the preferred one (or wrap to the smallest overall), and keep busy servers in a structure ordered by their free-at time so you can release any that have finished before handling the next arrival.

edge_inputs.description: wraparound assignment leaves one server clearly busiest
more servers than requests, several never get used
a request must be dropped because every server is still busy
a server frees up exactly at the next arrival's timestamp, which should count as available

stress.description: many requests over a modest server pool with strictly increasing arrivals
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    k = rng.randint(1, 100)
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    arrival = []
    t = 0
    for _ in range(n):
        t += rng.randint(1, 5)
        arrival.append(t)
    load = [rng.randint(1, 20) for _ in range(n)]
    return [k, arrival, load]
